paragraphs: Ambul Kesel is a Sri Lankan banana famous for being slightly sour while still sweet, a tangy profile that sets it apart from ordinary dessert bananas. It belongs to the Mysore (AAB) group. The fruit is petite and slender with sharp, tapered ends and a thin pale yellow skin when ripe, revealing soft, creamy flesh. At maturity it gives off a distinctive pungent, fruity aroma.

Its acidity makes Ambul a favourite for both fresh eating and cooking, including use when unripe and in banana chips. In Sri Lankan tradition it is regarded as a digestive aid and a cooling fruit. The name translates roughly as sour banana, reflecting the tang that defines it.

claims.text: Ambul Kesel is a Sri Lankan banana that is slightly sour yet sweet.
It belongs to the Mysore (AAB) group.
The fruit is petite and slender with sharp ends, thin skin and soft creamy flesh.
It emits a distinctive pungent aroma at maturity.
It is used both ripe and unripe, including for cooking and chips, and is regarded in Sri Lanka as a cooling, digestion-aiding fruit.

sources.name: Biodiversity for Food and Nutrition - Ambul banana
Roar Media - Guide to Sri Lankan Bananas

paragraphs: Blue Java is a triploid ABB hybrid of Musa balbisiana and Musa acuminata, widely known as the Ice Cream banana and also called the Hawaiian banana, Vanilla banana, Ney Mannan, Krie, or Cenizo. Its standout feature is the unripe fruit, which shows a silvery blue green colour caused by a heavy coat of wax, fading to pale yellow as it ripens.

The cultivar is named for its fruit, which has a sweet, aromatic, ice cream like consistency and a flavour reminiscent of vanilla. It is also valued for being cold tolerant and wind resistant, thanks to strong pseudostems and root systems, which makes it a popular choice in cooler subtropical gardens.

claims.text: Blue Java is a triploid ABB hybrid of Musa balbisiana and Musa acuminata, also called the Ice Cream banana, Hawaiian banana, Ney Mannan, Krie, or Cenizo.
The unripe fruit shows a silvery blue green colour caused by a heavy coat of wax.
The fruit has a sweet, aromatic, ice cream like consistency with a flavour reminiscent of vanilla.
It is cold tolerant and wind resistant due to strong pseudostems and root systems.

sources.name: Blue Java Ice Cream banana, nursery grounding

paragraphs: Cavendish is the subgroup of triploid AAA bananas that dominates world trade and Australian production. It rose to prominence from the 1950s after Panama disease (race 1) destroyed the previous export banana, Gros Michel. The name traces to William Cavendish, 6th Duke of Devonshire, whose gardener grew imported plants at Chatsworth in the 1830s. Named clones in the group include Dwarf Cavendish, Grand Nain and Williams.

Because Cavendish plants are grown as genetically identical clones with almost no genetic diversity, the whole crop shares the same weaknesses. It has no resistance to Fusarium wilt Tropical Race 4 (TR4), which is under active containment in North Queensland. In Australia over 95 percent of commercial plantation bananas are Cavendish, the bulk grown in North Queensland.

claims.text: Triploid AAA subgroup that dominates global banana trade and replaced Gros Michel after Panama disease race 1
Named after William Cavendish, 6th Duke of Devonshire, whose gardener grew imported plants at Chatsworth in the 1830s
Subgroup includes named clones such as Dwarf Cavendish, Grand Nain and Williams
Grown as genetically identical clones with very low genetic diversity
No resistance to Fusarium wilt Tropical Race 4, under containment in North Queensland
Over 95 percent of Australian commercial plantation bananas are Cavendish, mostly grown in North Queensland

sources.name: University of Queensland QAAFI: The world's favourite fruit needs a genetic boost

paragraphs: DPM25 is not a plantain but a Cavendish banana. It was developed by the then Queensland Department of Primary Industries in the early 1990s as a gamma irradiated mutant of the extra dwarf Cavendish cultivar Dwarf Parfitt. Belonging to the AAA Cavendish group, it resembles the standard commercial Williams closely in appearance and was selected for agronomic qualities comparable to it.

Its main point of interest is disease tolerance. DPM25 was chosen for increased tolerance to Panama disease subtropical race 4 (Foc SR4), the strain that affects Australian subtropical plantings. That tolerance does not carry across to the more aggressive tropical race 4 (Foc TR4). In Northern Territory field screening DPM25 rated as very susceptible to TR4, a reminder that resistance to one race of the pathogen does not guarantee protection against another.

claims.text: DPM25 was developed by the Queensland Department of Primary Industries in the early 1990s as a gamma irradiated mutant of the extra dwarf Cavendish cultivar Dwarf Parfitt
It is an AAA Cavendish selection that resembles Williams closely in appearance and was selected for agronomic qualities comparable to Williams
It was selected for increased tolerance to Panama disease subtropical race 4 (Foc SR4)
Its subtropical race 4 tolerance did not translate to tropical race 4, and it rated as very susceptible to Foc TR4 in Northern Territory field trials

sources.name: Banana Cultivar Field Screening for Resistance to Fusarium oxysporum f.sp. cubense Tropical Race 4 in the Northern Territory (PMC)
Australian Banana Growers' Council, Williams versus the rest

paragraphs: Ducasse is the Australian name for Pisang Awak, a triploid ABB banana also called the sugar banana and known as kluai nam wa in Thailand. It is one of the most widely grown bananas across Southeast Asia, valued for its sweet, fragrant flavour. The fruit is small and ripens to a bright yellow, and the plant suits backyard gardens at a moderate height.

The flesh is firm and resists browning, which makes Ducasse suitable for both fresh eating and cooking or processing. It tends to be a little more acid than Cavendish, and shows some tolerance to cooler growing areas, though frost should be avoided.

claims.text: Ducasse is the Australian name for Pisang Awak, a triploid ABB banana also called the sugar banana and kluai nam wa in Thailand.
The fruit is small, sweet and fragrant, and ripens to bright yellow.
The flesh is firm and resists browning, making it suitable for fresh eating and cooking.
It shows some tolerance to cooler growing areas, though frost should be avoided.

sources.name: Dwarf Banana Ducasse, Daleys, nursery grounding

paragraphs: Dwarf Cavendish is a specific short cultivar within the Cavendish (AAA) subgroup. The word dwarf refers to the height of the pseudostem, not the fruit, which is normal Cavendish size. Its compact, stable, wind-resistant habit makes it easier to manage and popular both for plantations and for home and container growing. The cultivar is associated with the Canary Islands and is one of the most widely planted Cavendish types.

An easy identifying trait is that the male bracts and flowers are retained on the plant rather than being shed. Young plants often show maroon or purple blotches on the leaves that fade with maturity.

claims.text: A specific short cultivar within the Cavendish AAA subgroup
Dwarf refers to pseudostem height, not fruit, which is normal Cavendish size
Compact, stable and wind-resistant habit suits plantations and home or container growing
Associated with the Canary Islands
Male bracts and flowers are retained rather than shed

paragraphs: Ducasse, widely sold in Australia as the sugar banana, is a small-fruited banana of the ABB sugar group (a Musa acuminata by Musa balbisiana hybrid related to Pisang Awak). Its fruit are petite with sweet, fragrant, creamy flesh that ripens to a bright yellow, prized for flavour rather than size.

The Dwarf Ducasse is a half-height selection of that same sugar banana, reaching only about 2 to 3 m rather than the taller full-size plant. The shorter stature suits courtyards, small gardens, and large pots, while the fruit stay the same small, sweet sugar bananas. It shows some tolerance of cooler subtropical conditions, though frost is best avoided.

claims.text: Ducasse (the sugar banana) is a small-fruited banana of the ABB sugar group, a Musa acuminata by Musa balbisiana hybrid, with sweet, fragrant, creamy flesh.
The dwarf selection reaches only about 2 to 3 m, roughly half the height of standard Ducasse, suiting small gardens and pots.
It tolerates some cooler subtropical conditions but frost should be avoided, and the fruit ripen bright yellow and creamy.

sources.name: Daleys Fruit Tree Nursery - Dwarf Banana Ducasse

paragraphs: Dwarf Ducasse is a compact form of the Ducasse, the Australian name for the Thai banana Pisang Awak (an ABB triploid). Widely called the sugar banana, it was introduced to northern Australia from Thailand in the 1880s and proved more adaptable than Cavendish. The fruit is small to medium with a thin, smooth, angular peel and rich, sweet flesh that is firm and starchy, turning soft and dense when fully ripe.

The dwarf form is a stout natural mutant of Pisang Awak (known overseas as Dwarf Namwah), making a shorter, more wind tolerant plant suited to home gardens. In Australia the Dwarf Ducasse is restricted to the south east Queensland banana growing region under quarantine rules.

claims.text: Ducasse is the Australian name for the Thai banana Pisang Awak, an ABB triploid.
It is commonly called the sugar banana and was introduced to northern Australia from Thailand in the 1880s.
The flesh is sweet, firm and starchy with a thin smooth peel.
The dwarf form is a stout mutant of Pisang Awak, known overseas as Dwarf Namwah.
In Australia the Dwarf Ducasse is restricted to the south east Queensland banana growing region.

sources.name: Fruitopia Nursery - Dwarf Duccase / Sugar Banana

paragraphs: French plantain names a major type within the true plantains, the AAB cooking bananas grown as a staple across West and Central Africa. Plantains are divided into four groups by their bunch form: French, French Horn, False Horn and Horn.

What sets the French type apart is its bunch: it keeps persistent bracts and a large male bud at the tip, and carries many hands of comparatively small fruit, where Horn types shed the male bud and bear fewer, larger fingers. The fruit are starchy and low in sugar, so they are cooked (fried, boiled or baked) rather than eaten raw.

claims.text: True plantains are AAB cooking bananas divided into four groups by bunch type: French, French Horn, False Horn and Horn.
French types keep persistent bracts and a large male bud and bear large bunches of many hands with relatively small fruit, unlike Horn types which lose the male bud and bear fewer, larger fruit.
Plantains are starchy, low-sugar bananas eaten cooked rather than raw, and are a dietary staple in Africa.

paragraphs: Gold Finger, formally FHIA-01, is a tetraploid AAAB hybrid dessert banana developed at the Honduran Foundation for Agricultural Research (FHIA) by a team led by Phillip Rowe and Franklin Rosales, with the subacid Brazilian clone Prata-ana in its parentage. It was bred specifically as a more disease resistant alternative to vulnerable varieties like Cavendish.

Its main appeal is resilience: it is resistant to black Sigatoka, Race 4 of Panama disease and burrowing nematode, and is more tolerant of cold and wind than most bananas. This makes it well suited to subtropical conditions. The ripe fruit is sweet with a slightly tart, apple like flavour, and the plant takes longer than many bananas to mature.

claims.text: Gold Finger (FHIA-01) is a tetraploid AAAB hybrid dessert banana developed at the Honduran Foundation for Agricultural Research (FHIA) by a team led by Phillip Rowe and Franklin Rosales.
It is resistant to black Sigatoka, Race 4 of Panama disease and burrowing nematode, and is more tolerant of cold and wind than most bananas.
The ripe fruit is sweet with a slightly tart, apple like flavour.

sources.name: Goldfinger (FHIA-01) Banana Grow Guide, nursery grounding

paragraphs: Horn Plantain belongs to the AAB plantain group, one of four plantain bunch types (French, French Horn, False Horn and Horn). It is a true cooking banana, not a dessert type, with the classic starchy plantain character that is eaten cooked rather than raw. The fruit is large and the male bud degenerates as the bunch matures, a trait shared with the related False Horn group.

Horn types are known for sparse bunches, typically carrying very few large fingers spread across only one to five hands (occasionally up to eight or ten). The starchy flesh is versatile in the kitchen, fried, boiled or roasted, and can be used green or ripe. Related Horn cultivars from Asia include Tanduk and Pisang Tandok.

claims.text: Horn Plantain is an AAB plantain, one of four plantain bunch types (French, French Horn, False Horn, Horn).
In Horn plantains the male bud degenerates at maturity.
Horn types carry very few fruit spread over one to five hands (exceptionally eight to ten).
Plantains are starchy and normally eaten cooked rather than raw.
Related Horn cultivars include Tanduk and Pisang Tandok.

paragraphs: In Australia the name Lady Finger refers to a Pome subgroup banana (AAB group, a Musa acuminata by balbisiana hybrid), not the AA Sucrier sold under that name elsewhere. Pome bananas have a sub-acid, apple-like character, and Lady Finger is prized as sweeter and more flavoursome than Cavendish. It is the second most popular banana in Australia after Cavendish, grown mainly in NSW with more recent plantings on the Atherton Tablelands.
Its main drawback is disease. Lady Finger is highly susceptible to Panama disease (Fusarium wilt race 1), which Cavendish resists, and it is also affected by subtropical race 4.

claims.text: In Australia Lady Finger is a Pome subgroup banana (AAB group, Musa acuminata x balbisiana hybrid)
Pome bananas have a sub-acid, apple-like character
Regarded as sweeter and more flavoursome than Cavendish
Second most popular banana in Australia after Cavendish, grown mainly in NSW with plantings on the Atherton Tablelands
Highly susceptible to Panama disease (Fusarium wilt race 1), which Cavendish resists, and affected by subtropical race 4

sources.name: Better Bananas (Australian banana industry): Panama disease research and development

paragraphs: In Australian nurseries, 'Pacific Plantain' is a Pacific Island cooking banana of the Maoli-Popoulu group (AAB), bananas domesticated long ago in the Pacific, probably between the Bismarck Archipelago and the Solomon Islands. Together with the related Iholena bananas they are often called Pacific plantains.

The group is recognised by its plump, sausage-shaped fruit (Popoulu types fatter and shorter, Maoli types longer). The flesh is starchy and lower in sugar than dessert bananas, so it is used cooked, fried, baked or grilled until it caramelises. In Australia these Maia Maoli/Popoulu types are grown mainly in north Queensland.

claims.text: Pacific plantains are the Maoli-Popoulu (and related Iholena) cooking bananas of the AAB group, domesticated in the Pacific, possibly the Bismarck Archipelago to Solomon Islands area.
The cultivars are recognised by their sausage-shaped fruit; Popoulu fruit are fatter and shorter than the longer Maoli fruit.
Sold in Australia as Pacific Plantain, it is a starchy cooking banana lower in sugar than dessert types, eaten fried, baked or grilled; these Maia Maoli/Popoulu types are grown in north Queensland.

sources.name: RFCA Archives - Some Banana Varieties of the South Pacific
Daleys Fruit Tree Nursery - Pacific Plantain

paragraphs: Pisang Ceylon is a Mysore type banana, part of the AAB Mysore subgroup that originates from India and Pakistan. Mysore bananas are among the most widely grown cultivars in India, traditionally valued there as a fresh eating variety. The fruit is small and thin skinned, in the lady finger style.
The flesh is sweet, and the small fruits are borne in dense bunches on a tall, sturdy plant. Like other Mysore types it is a dessert banana eaten fresh rather than a cooking banana.

claims.text: Pisang Ceylon is a Mysore type banana in the AAB Mysore subgroup, originating from India and Pakistan.
The fruit is small, thin skinned and sweet, in the lady finger style.
Mysore types are among the most widely grown bananas in India and are eaten fresh.

sources.name: List of banana cultivars (Mysore subgroup, AAB) - Wikipedia

paragraphs: Red Dacca is a red-skinned dessert banana, a triploid cultivar of Musa acuminata in the AAA group. Where most supermarket bananas are yellow Cavendish, Red Dacca ripens to a deep red or maroon rind, making it instantly recognisable. The fruit is often shorter and plumper than a Cavendish, with a slightly thicker skin. It is also known by names such as Cuban Red and Jamaican Red.

The creamy flesh is softer and sweeter than Cavendish, and the flavour is sometimes described as carrying a tangy raspberry note. It is eaten fresh and used in desserts and smoothies, prized more for its colour and dessert quality than for cooking.

claims.text: Red Dacca is a red-skinned dessert banana, a triploid cultivar of Musa acuminata in the AAA group.
It ripens to a deep red or maroon rind and is often shorter and plumper than a Cavendish.
The flesh is softer and sweeter than Cavendish, sometimes with a tangy raspberry note.

paragraphs: Saba is the Philippines' most important cooking banana, a robust triploid of the ABB group (a Musa acuminata by Musa balbisiana hybrid). The plants are large and sturdy, with thick pseudostems reaching roughly 6 to 9 m, carrying heavy bunches of many hands.

Its fruit are unmistakable: short, squarish and angular with thick skin and dense, white, starchy flesh. That starchiness makes Saba a cooking banana first (the base for Filipino banana cue and turon), though ripe fruit can be eaten fresh. Its strong Musa balbisiana parentage lends tolerance of drier soils and cooler conditions, and it is also known as the cardaba or cardava banana.

claims.text: Saba is a triploid ABB cooking banana originating in the Philippines, a Musa acuminata by Musa balbisiana hybrid.
The plants are very large and robust, with pseudostems reaching about 6 to 9 m.
Fruit are short, squarish and angular with thick skin and white, starchy flesh.
It is primarily a cooking banana (used for banana cue and turon) but can be eaten raw when ripe, and the B genome lends tolerance of drier soil and cooler conditions; also known as cardaba/cardava.

paragraphs: Super Dwarf Cavendish is a very compact selection within the Cavendish group of dessert bananas (triploid AAA), descended from the same commercial stock as the common supermarket banana. It is one of the shortest banana selections in cultivation, which is why it is prized as a tub plant for patios, balconies and small gardens, and is even grown indoors. The short pseudostem keeps the plant stable and wind resistant, and brings the bunch down to an easy height to manage and harvest.

Like other Dwarf Cavendish types it carries broad leaves on short petioles, and the fruit eats much like a standard Cavendish, just borne on a far smaller plant. A distinguishing Cavendish quirk it shares is that the spent male bracts and flowers are not shed from the bunch, an easy field marker for the group.

claims.text: A very compact selection of the Cavendish dessert banana group (triploid AAA), descended from commercial Cavendish stock.
One of the shortest banana selections, so it is stable and wind resistant and suits containers, balconies and indoors.
Produces fruit similar to a standard Cavendish, on a much smaller plant.
Carries broad leaves on short petioles, and the male bracts and flowers are not shed from the bunch.

sources.name: Bananas.org wiki, Musa Super Dwarf Cavendish

paragraphs: The variety sold in Australia as Tonga is one of the Hopa bananas. In Tonga, Hopa is the local name for bananas of the Maoli and Popoulu types, a group domesticated in the Pacific (possibly around the Bismarck Archipelago and Solomon Islands) and carried across Polynesia in ancient times. They belong to the AAB genome group, the Maoli-Popoulu subgroup, which together with the Iholena bananas is often called the Pacific plantains. Australian nurseries list the plant as Musa acuminata x balbisiana, consistent with that hybrid AAB makeup.

The fruit are recognised by their fat, sausage shaped fingers with bluntly squared ends, the Popoulu types running shorter and fatter than the Maoli. They are dual purpose. Starchy and firm when green, they are used much like a cooking banana, then turn soft and sweet for fresh eating once ripe. The type is rarely seen outside Polynesia and is now grown mainly in Tonga, largely in home gardens and local markets rather than as a commercial export crop.

claims.text: In Tonga, Hopa is the local name for bananas of the Maoli and Popoulu types, the group sold in Australia under the name Tonga
They belong to the AAB genome group in the Maoli-Popoulu subgroup, which with the Iholena bananas is known as the Pacific plantains
The subgroup was domesticated in the Pacific, possibly the Bismarck Archipelago and Solomon Islands area, and spread across Polynesia
Australian nurseries list the variety as Musa acuminata x balbisiana, consistent with its AAB hybrid makeup
The fruit are sausage shaped with bluntly squared ends, the Popoulu types shorter and fatter than the Maoli
They are dual purpose, starchy and used like a cooking banana when green, then soft and sweet for fresh eating when ripe
The type is rarely seen outside Polynesia and is now grown mainly in Tonga, largely in home gardens and local markets

sources.name: Daleys Fruit Tree Nursery, Banana Tonga

paragraphs: William (the Williams banana) is the dominant commercial Cavendish grown in Australia. It arose around 1910 as a tall mutant in a Dwarf Cavendish (Chinese Cavendish) plantation in Australia, and its greater height and yield saw it progressively replace the dwarf type, becoming the country's leading banana cultivar by the 1950s. It is a triploid (AAA) Musa acuminata, so the fruit is seedless and sweet, eaten fresh as the classic dessert banana.

Williams is the benchmark variety for the Australian industry, valued for a fast crop cycle (a short time from planting to harvest) and large, cylindrical bunches. It belongs to the taller Giant Cavendish clones, well above Dwarf Cavendish in height, which makes commercial bunches easier to handle. Backyard growers choose it for reliable, familiar-flavoured Cavendish fruit.

claims.text: The dominant commercial Cavendish and benchmark banana variety grown in Australia.
Arose around 1910 as a tall mutant in a Dwarf Cavendish plantation in Australia and replaced the dwarf type, becoming the leading cultivar by the 1950s.
A triploid AAA Musa acuminata, giving seedless, sweet dessert fruit.
Valued for a fast crop cycle and large cylindrical bunches; the Australian industry standard.
Belongs to the Giant Cavendish clones, taller than Dwarf Cavendish.

sources.name: Australian Banana Growers' Council, Williams versus the rest
